deductions.evidence: In the image, 'SCM' is a single filled-circle control terminal on the drive's terminal block, listed in a vertical column with +24V, STO1, STO2 — it is a terminal label, not a device. The image's actual distinct safety device (a labeled rectangular box with an internal switch/contact symbol) is clearly printed as 'Safety PLC', wired between E STOP and the drive's STO1/STO2/SCM/+24V terminals. The candidate never mentions 'Safety PLC' anywhere in the response.
deductions.correction: Identify the labeled box between E STOP and the drive terminals as 'Safety PLC' (an external safety controller). Describe 'SCM' as the drive's Safety Common terminal, not as a module/device with an expanded functional name.

deductions.claim: The digital inputs (DI3 to DI7) are configured for multi-step speed control.
deductions.evidence: The default-I/O legend box in the image maps DI3->'Multi-step speed 1', DI4->'Multi-step speed 2', DI5->'Multi-step speed 3', DI6->'Multi-step speed 4', but DI7's default row reads 'N/A', with a separate footnote '*DI7 can input 33 kHz pulses.' DI7 is not part of the multi-step-speed group by default.
deductions.correction: State that DI3-DI6 default to multi-step speed 1-4, and DI7 defaults to N/A (unused), optionally configurable as a 33 kHz pulse input.

deductions.evidence: The image's relay-output label reads 'R1O' (letter O, matching 'Multi-function relay output terminals'), not 'R10' (digit zero) as written by the candidate.
deductions.correction: Transcribe the terminal as R1O, not R10.

deductions.claim: (Omission) No mention of the optional DC reactor, optional brake resistor, or the manual's recommended R1C-R1 protective circuit note.
deductions.evidence: The image explicitly labels 'DC reactor (optional)' and 'Brake resistor (optional)' at the top of the main power path, and a boxed NOTE recommends installing a protective circuit at R1C-R1 to protect the system, describing that an MC contact switches to ON to shut off power on a fault. None of this is addressed in the candidate's power/control-flow explanation.
deductions.correction: Mention the optional DC reactor/brake resistor taps and the recommended R1C-R1 protective circuit (MC contact opens power on fault) as part of the power-path explanation.

deductions.claim: The emergency stop (E STOP) is connected to the safety circuit module (SCM), which is linked to STO1 and STO2.
deductions.evidence: The image shows E STOP wired into the 'Safety PLC' box; the Safety PLC's internal switch/contact output is what wires to STO1 and STO2 (with a separate +24VDC supply feeding the +24V/SCM node). The candidate's description skips the Safety PLC entirely and implies 'SCM' itself performs the switching/interlock function between E-STOP and STO1/STO2, which is a materially incorrect account of the interlock chain.
deductions.correction: Describe the chain as: E STOP -> Safety PLC (external safety relay/controller) -> Safety PLC output contact wired to STO1/STO2, with SCM and +24V as separate terminals the Safety PLC/supply also lands on.

deductions.claim: The digital inputs (DI3 to DI7) are configured for multi-step speed control.
deductions.evidence: Grouping DI7 with DI3-DI6 under one sequencing function (multi-step speed) misstates the interlock/sequence table shown in the legend, where DI7 is a separate, unused-by-default input (N/A / optional 33 kHz pulse input) rather than a fifth speed-step input.
deductions.correction: Describe only DI3-DI6 as the four multi-step-speed selection inputs; describe DI7 separately as N/A by default.

deductions.claim: SCM (safety circuit module) -- listed as a distinct 'control circuit component' and later as the device linking E-STOP to STO1/STO2.
deductions.evidence: No text in the image expands 'SCM' or labels it a 'module.' It is one terminal among several (SCM, +24V, STO1, STO2) on the drive's control-terminal strip. The only distinctly-boxed safety device visible and legible in the print is labeled 'Safety PLC.' The candidate invents a device name/identity for SCM that is not supported by the print, while never reporting the device that actually is shown.
deductions.correction: Do not expand SCM into an invented device name. Report the legible 'Safety PLC' label as the external safety device, and SCM as a drive terminal only.

deductions.claim: Nothing major appears to be unreadable in the provided diagram. All critical labels and connections seem clear based on the OCR data provided.
deductions.evidence: This blanket completeness claim is inconsistent with the response's own content: the clearly legible 'Safety PLC' label and box were not captured or reported at all, and the DI7 'N/A' legend entry was misread as multi-step speed. The response presents its own extraction as complete and fully grounded when it demonstrably missed legible, in-scope content.
deductions.correction: Avoid a blanket 'everything is clear/captured' statement; if a device or legend entry was not incorporated, that is a recognition gap, not evidence of full legibility coverage.

deductions.claim: Overall explanation of the safety interlock circuit (SCM/E-STOP/STO1/STO2) presented as settled fact for a technician to rely on.
deductions.evidence: Because the response substitutes an invented 'safety circuit module' for the actual visible 'Safety PLC' device and omits it entirely, a technician using this explanation to troubleshoot a safety-stop fault would not know to check/reset the external Safety PLC, creating false confidence about the safety architecture on a safety-critical circuit.
deductions.correction: Explicitly flag the Safety PLC as the external safety controller a technician needs to check, distinct from the drive's own STO/SCM terminals.

notes: Independent second pass, no prior grade file read. Two material findings drove the score: (1) the print's clearly-labeled external 'Safety PLC' device is never mentioned, and 'SCM' (a drive terminal) is instead mischaracterized as a 'safety circuit module' that links E-STOP to STO1/STO2 -- a materially incorrect account of the safety interlock topology on a safety-critical circuit. (2) DI7 is grouped with DI3-DI6 as 'multi-step speed control' inputs, but the print's default-I/O legend explicitly marks DI7 as 'N/A' (optional 33 kHz pulse input), not a speed-step input. Both are visible, legible facts in the image that the candidate got wrong with no hedging.
